html:
Do not confuse with the botanist Magnus Fries.

Elias Magnus Fries, born on August 15, 1794, in Femsjö, and died on February 8, 1878, in Uppsala, was a Swedish botanist and mycologist. He was a professor at Uppsala University and a member of the Swedish Academy from 1847 to 1878 (chair 14), as well as rector magnificus of Uppsala University in 1839 and again from 1853 to 1854. In addition to mycology, he was particularly interested in algae, flowering plants, and ferns.

Fries is primarily known for his systematic work on fungi, "Systema mycologicum" (three parts, 1821–1832), and is considered one of the founders of modern mycology. In the National Portrait Collection at Gripsholm Castle, there is a bust of Fries created by Johan Gustaf Sandberg in 1838. He is buried in the old cemetery in Uppsala.

== Biography == Fries was the son of the pastor, provost Thore Fries (1762–1839) from Femsjö in Småland, and Sara Elisabeth (Sara-Lisa) Wernelin (1769–1837). His family, known as the "Eliassläkten" or "Elias lineage," descended from Adam Jonsson Fries (1640–1710), a blacksmith from Norrköping. The name "Frijs" entered the family with Adam Jonsson and continued with his son Thore Adamsson (1683–1732) – variations included "Fris," "Friese," "Frijs," or "Fries."

Fries initially studied botany at Lund University, where he was a student of Anders Jahan Retzius and Carl Adolph Agardh. In 1814, he became a docent (lecturer) in botany in Lund, and in 1819, he received a personal position as an assistant professor of botany. Fries obtained the title of professor in 1824 and became a demonstrator of botany in 1828. In 1834, he left Lund for Uppsala, where he was appointed to the Borgström professorship in practical economics (from 1851, botany and practical economics). He also became the director of the botanical garden and museum there. In 1859, he retired from his professorship but remained as director of the botanical garden until 1863. His major work on fungal classification, *Systema mycologicum*, was published in three parts between 1821 and 1832, and is considered an important contribution to the development of modern mycology. Fries's work on lichens, *Lichenographia europæa reformata* (1831), earned him the Linnaean Gold Medal. He was elected as member number 392 of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in 1821. In 1847, he became a member of the Swedish Academy and in 1868, he received an honorary doctorate from Lund University.

He also served as a Member of Parliament, representing Uppsala University at the Riksdag (Swedish parliament) in 1844–1845 and 1847–1848, and was a member of the constitutional committee. Fries was also a board member of Ultuna Agricultural Institute from 1846 to 1859 and served on a committee for drafting new university statutes. During his time in Uppsala, Fries was the inspector of Smålands nation (a student society). Elias Fries married Kristina Wieslander (1809–1862), the daughter of a pastor, in 1832. His son, Teodor (Thore) Magnus Fries, became a professor of botany, and his sons Elias Petrus and Oscar Robert both continued their studies in mycology. The son Elias Petrus, as well as the daughter Susanna Kristina, both illustrated fungi, and several original drawings by them are preserved in Uppsala.

== Selected Bibliography == Novitiæ floræ svecicæ (1814–24, new edition 1828) Observationes mycologicæ (1815–18) Flora hallandica (1817–18) Systema mycologicum (3 volumes, 1821–29) Stirpes agri femsionensis (1825–27) Elenchus fungorum (1828) Lichenographia europæa reformata (1831) Flora scanica (1835–37) Epicrisis systematis mycologici (1836–38, new edition 1874) Botaniska utflygter (3 volumes, 1843–64) Summa vegetabilium Scandinaviæ (1846–49) Symbolae ad historiam Hieraciorum (1847) Monographia Hymenomycetum Sueciæ (2 volumes, 1857–63) Sveriges ätliga och giftiga svampar (1860–66) Epicrisis generis Hieraciorum (1862) Icones selectæ Hymenomycentum (1867–84) Kritisk ordbok öfver svenska växtnamnen (1880)

== Children == Theodor (Thore) Magnus Elias (1832–1913), professor of botany at Uppsala University from 1859. Married to Gustava Katarina Anjou (1844–1927). Elias Petrus (1834–1858). Held a Master's degree in mycology and was a skilled mushroom illustrator; 70 of his illustrations are preserved at Uppsala University. Sara (Sally) Lovisa Ulrika (1836–1905). Married to Carl Robert Stellan Mörner (1833–1875). Johan Otto (1838–1931), surveyor. Emigrated to the USA in 1871, where he sent a great deal of plant material to the botanical institute at Uppsala University. Oscar Robert (1840–1908), medical doctor and mycologist. He married Sofia Elisabeth Bergman (1853–1895) on November 9, 1872, and a square in Gothenburg is named after him: Doktor Fries Torg (1949). Susanna (Sanna) Kristina (1841–1919). She illustrated mushrooms, and seven original illustrations remain at Uppsala University. Married to August Almén (1833–1903). Linnéa Johanna (1841–1846). Junia Gustava Elisabeth (1845–1906).

== Author Abbreviation == The author abbreviation "Fr." can be used for Elias Fries in connection with a scientific name within botany; see Wikipedia articles that link to the author abbreviation.

== Further reading ==

How certain can we be about this?

Information about individuals from the 1800s is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The main uncertainties relate to selection bias and perspective – whose stories are being highlighted.
tldr: Elias Fries was a significant Swedish researcher during the 19th century, renowned for his studies of plants, particularly fungi. He is considered one of the founders of modern mycology (the scientific study of fungi).
lattlast:
Elias Fries was born in Småland in 1794 and worked for many years as a teacher and researcher at the University of Uppsala. He was an expert on plants, algae, and fungi. His extensive book on how to classify different types of fungi is still well-known in science today.

In addition to his work with plants, he was also active in society. He served in parliament for several years and helped establish regulations for the university. He also received numerous prestigious awards for his studies of lichens and other plant types.

Elias Fries had a large family, and many of his children also became experts on plants or fungi. One of his sons became a doctor and has a square named after him in Gothenburg.
